it be something to get so far , though we have not power to go further . we have show before , that the true felicity of man , or his sovereign good be enjoy in the life to come : and that there be no estate of this worldly life void of no estate free from calamity . trouble and calamity . for as there be no sea without wave , no more be there any kind of life without trouble : so that we must be drive to call that life , felicity , which have in it least misery , and he to be happy , that feel least trouble and calamity , and best bear they . for there be never any man to who some thing either have not happen , or may happen , to make he sometime sorrowful . but he that make least account of it , be wise and happy , as one say : he• … est est enim mortalium foelicitas , dolere quàm fieri potest rarissime . for this be the felicity of man , to feel sorrow as seldom as may be . London print by George eld ▪ 1612. to the right honourable the lord Viscont Cranborn . my good lord : i have presume to offer this discourse unto your view , not for that you understand it not in the original , or to instruct your knowledge , the french tongue be familiar unto you , and the subject , with all the circumstance , as well know as to any ; but to make you censor of the work , and of the author integrity , have remain in that court , and receive favour from that great king , live in the height of worldly felicity , and with all be a eye witness of that sad and mournful spectacle , and of the general consternation of the french , lament the tragical and sudden death of their king , to their irreparable loss . this work may happy seem to some unseasonable , when as his death be in a manner forget , there have also be so many pamphlet , and petty discourse publish of that subject . but as my author excuse be , that time can prescribe no bound to their sorrow , nor reason moderate their grief , which renew with time , and therefore never unseasonable to lament so great a loss . so i find it write by a eloquent pen , and by his own historiographer , who attend on he daily , to record both his word and action , and who both can and have write many particularity upon this accident , which be unknown to other , have think it as worthy the knowledge of our nation as any of the rest , and i hope will give the reader more content . i have presume to make your lordship patron of my labour , both for that you can judge if there be any thing defective , and in respect of my own private obligation , have receive many favour from your lordship during my abode in France , with that hopeful gentleman master Henry Howard : vouchsafe it my good lord your countenance , and accept it as a poor testimony of his thankfulness , who be and will be always ready to do you service : ed : Grimeston .
